text: Hi from Munich and welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Thomas
from Vitesco Technologies. Hi Thomas. Hi David, thanks for having me. Sure. Thomas,
can you tell us about Vitesco Technologies? Vitesco Technologies is a leading international
developer and manufacturer of state-of-the-art powertrain technologies for sustainable mobility.
I know that Vitesco Technologies is a carve-out or spin-off of a larger corporation, right? Can you
share something about the challenges that you were faced with regarding your infrastructure in the IT?
That's right. With the spin-off we had the challenge to separate the IT landscape of over 50 locations
with more than 1,000 servers, 300 databases and more than 300 applications in just two years. And
that's why we have decided to build the so-called Cloud Foundation as one of the first things. The
Cloud Foundation is an end-to-end automated cloud lending zone. It allows our internal users to create
AWS accounts directly and very fast while ensuring that they are configured according to our standards
and AWS best practices. Since the release we have already created 850 accounts that way. Okay, that's
quite a lot of accounts, right? And I assume there were quite some challenges you faced with regarding
governance and all that. Let's dive a little bit into the architecture and let's assume I am a user at
Vitesco Technologies and I want to get an AWS account. How does the experience there look like?
Mm-hmm. The user journey begins with our self-developed web portal. Here they select the desired network
configuration, additional blueprints and put in other metadata like the development stage. This request
will then be sent through an API gateway to this AWS step function. We are calling this step function
the account vending machine because it holds all necessary steps to provide the final account.
And one of the first steps is to create the actual account through AWS Control Tower. Since Control Tower does
not provide an API at the moment, this is happening through a custom service catalog item. Okay, I understand.
I see a database here which is a DynamoDB table I assume. Can you share what that exactly is?
Mm-hmm. In the DynamoDB, we store all information that describes the target state of the account.
So this can include user input and also other information which we generate on the fly,
like the VPC CIDA range or the account IDs. This is especially important to allow future changes
changes and ensure the item potency of the whole process. So no matter how often this runs,
the DynamoDB always reflects the reality of the deployed accounts.
Okay, that's really interesting. You mentioned something you call blueprints at the beginning,
something that users can select in the portal, right? What exactly are those and how do they play into the
whole picture? Yeah. The blueprints describe all configurations which we do in the deployed accounts.
So for example, there is the IAM
and the security blueprint.
These are obligated. Here we are doing things like configuring the IAM identity provider,
configuring the security services like security hub, guard duty and AWS config, as well as configuring
the aggregation into the central security account. Other blueprints depends on the user selection. So for
example, the network blueprints.
Here, this can include VPC and subnet configuration, Route 53 setup, transit gateway attachment,
and much more. Okay, I understand. This is basically what the user selects here on the...
That's right. Okay. And now, once all of those blueprints are rolled out, what happens next?
Do the user, does the user get a notification or how does it work? After everything is deployed into the
account and ready to go, we are notifying the user through SCS.
Yes. This mail contains how to access and how to use the account and also points out the account
owner's responsibilities. Okay. So you previously mentioned security configurations and networking
configurations, right? So my question would be what happens if you centrally want to change something?
Let's assume from your cloud platform team you want to add an additional security guardrail or you want to
change a networking route in the transit gateway. Is there any way to handle that?
Yeah. That happens often. Our goal is that each change on the blueprints reaches the customer account
in less than three hours. That's possible because every change triggers a code pipeline and this code
pipeline deploys directly into the customer accounts. And this deployment is always done with respect to
the customer's development stage. So we deployed to dev accounts first, then QA accounts and finally to
prod accounts. Okay. That's really great. I like how you have a notion of the stage in each account that
you make sure that you don't cause any problems in the production accounts, right? So finally, do you want to
share a bit about what you think is the coolest part about the solution? Yeah, for me, that's really the
great of automation we achieved here. By utilizing all these managed services, we are able to operate
the complete cloud foundation with a platform team of only four engineers and they are still able to do
further improvement of the platform and add more self-service capabilities. Oh yeah, that's really
impressive. So thanks so much, Thomas, for sharing your solution here. You're welcome. And thank you for watching
This is my architecture.
